ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03301056 maps to 'cookie' as a baked sweet biscuit product; mini format is a size variant not separately coded.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for mini cookie; SNOMED CT does not enumerate individual snack sub-formats at this granularity.

serving.natural_language_portion: One mini cookie weighs approximately 10 g, providing about 47 kcal; a typical snack serving of 3–4 mini cookies delivers 140–190 kcal.

nutrient_source.title: Cookie, butter, commercially prepared — HPB Singapore Foodo / USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 18158)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy 470 kcal, protein 6 g, fat 17.5 g, carbohydrate 73 g, sugar 30.5 g, sodium 310 mg, dietary fibre 0 g) are taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo record for Cookie (mini), CR_ID F11080017, confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated at 9.0 g/100 g based on USDA SR Legacy NDB 18158 (butter cookie, commercially prepared: saturated fat 8.9 g/100 g), which is the closest approved-source analogue. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, potassium, cholesterol, vitamins, and minerals not reported in HPB record; USDA SR Legacy values used where available and documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal (peer-reviewed)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 55 is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database for plain sweet biscuit/cookie (glucose reference = 100). The input record cited GI 55 from an embedded GI database consistent with this peer-reviewed reference. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 7.3 g per 10 g serving) / 100 = 4.0. Input record GL of 40.1 appears to have been calculated per 100 g rather than per serving; corrected to per-serving basis (10 g) per template requirement.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar (30.5 g/100 g) with zero dietary fibre drives insulin-mediated sodium retention; sodium at 310 mg/100 g provides no offsetting benefit, and the mini format encourages overconsumption.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid mini cookies due to the combination of high sugar (30.5 g/100 g), zero fibre, and 310 mg sodium/100 g, which together promote fluid retention and elevate blood pressure; if consumed, limit to one or two pieces and pair with a high-potassium food.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar at 30.5 g/100 g exceeds the 20 g/100 g glycaemic ceiling; carbohydrate is 73 g/100 g with zero dietary fibre, producing a very high glycaemic load and rapid postprandial glucose spike.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Mini cookies are not suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes — sugar at 30.5 g/100 g, carbohydrate at 73 g/100 g, and zero fibre create a high glycaemic load profile that will cause rapid blood glucose elevation; this food should be avoided entirely for blood glucose management.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat approximately 9 g/100 g and trans fat approximately 0.3 g/100 g from shortening raise LDL cholesterol; high sugar (30.5 g/100 g) with zero fibre promotes dyslipidaemia and endothelial inflammation.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid mini cookies because the combination of saturated fat (~9 g/100 g), trans fat (~0.3 g/100 g), high sugar (30.5 g/100 g), and zero dietary fibre raises LDL cholesterol, promotes triglyceride elevation, and contributes to endothelial dysfunction.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Trans fat (~0.3 g/100 g from shortening) is a hard pro-inflammatory trigger; saturated fat (~9 g/100 g) and sugar (30.5 g/100 g) with zero fibre further promote inflammatory cytokine production.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid mini cookies entirely, as trans fat from shortening, high saturated fat, and high sugar with zero fibre collectively promote systemic inflammation and may worsen joint symptoms.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mini cookies are bite-sized, commercially baked sweet biscuits typically made from wheat flour, sugar, butter or vegetable shortening, eggs, salt, and chemical leavening agents. The mini format — approximately 10 g per piece — is a size variant of the standard cookie, designed for snacking, party platters, and portion-controlled indulgence, though in practice the small size often encourages mindless overconsumption. Nutritionally, mini cookies are energy-dense at 470 kcal per 100 g, with a high carbohydrate load of 73 g/100 g, of which 30.5 g is sugar, and zero dietary fibre. Fat content is 17.5 g/100 g, with saturated fat estimated at approximately 9 g/100 g from butter or shortening, and trace trans fat (~0.3 g/100 g) from partially hydrogenated vegetable shortening. Sodium is 310 mg/100 g. The glycemic index is approximately 55 (low), but the very high carbohydrate density and zero fibre produce a high effective glycaemic impact per eating occasion. Clinically, mini cookies are unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes (sugar far exceeds the 20 g/100 g ceiling), hypertension (high sugar drives insulin-mediated sodium retention; sodium 310 mg/100 g), cardiovascular disease (saturated and trans fat raise LDL), and rheumatoid arthritis (trans fat and high sugar are pro-inflammatory). The HPB Singapore Foodo database classifies this food under Snacks > Biscuits, cookies and crackers with high confidence (0.95).

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo record F11080017 with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, potassium, cholesterol, and water estimated from USDA SR Legacy NDB 18158 (butter cookie, commercially prepared), the closest approved-source analogue. GI of 55 corroborated by University of Sydney GI Database for plain sweet biscuit/cookie. All sources are approved under Project Veritas v2 source policy.
notes: 1. Dietary fibre is 0.0 g/100 g — confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo source; refined wheat flour cookies with no added fibre ingredients are expected to have negligible fibre. 2. Calcium is 0.0 mg/100 g and iron is 0.0 mg/100 g — confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo source; these values are plausible for a refined-flour cookie without mineral fortification, though USDA SR Legacy reports small amounts (calcium ~14 mg, iron ~1.7 mg/100 g for butter cookie); HPB source value retained as primary. 3. Saturated fat 9.0 g/100 g, monounsaturated fat 5.1 g/100 g, polyunsaturated_fat_g 1.4 g/100 g, trans_fat_g 0.3 g/100 g, potassium 68 mg/100 g, cholesterol 35 mg/100 g, and water 4.0 g/100 g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy NDB 18158 (butter cookie, commercially prepared) as the closest approved-source analogue; these values are not reported in the HPB source. 4. Vitamin C, vitamin A, B-vitamins, vitamin D, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, and selenium are set to -1 (data not available): detailed micronutrient data for this specific mini cookie format is not reported in either the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA SR Legacy at the level of confidence required for a canonical record; follow-up with USDA FoodData Central branded food entries for commercial mini cookie products is recommended. 5. Glycemic load per serving corrected to 4.0 (per 10 g serving) from the input value of 40.1, which appears to have been calculated per 100 g rather than per serving. 6. Trans fat ~0.3 g/100 g is estimated from USDA SR Legacy analogue; actual trans fat content will vary by manufacturer and formulation. 7. Halal status is marked false as this cannot be confirmed without manufacturer certification; the product may contain lard-based shortening in some formulations.
